title: Fried plantain
subtitle: Sweet ripe plantain fried golden in oil
excerpt: Ripe plantain sliced and shallow-fried in vegetable oil until golden and caramelised on both sides. Soft inside, slightly crisp at the edges, sweetly fragrant. The universal Caribbean side dish.
sourceNotes: Fried plantain (maduros or sweet fried plantain) appears across the Caribbean, West Africa, and Central America. The Caribbean version most commonly uses very ripe, almost black-skinned plantain, which is sweeter and softer than the green plantain used for tostones. In the UK, plantain is available in Caribbean and African food shops year-round. This recipe uses banana as a substitute where plantain is unavailable, though the flavour is milder and the texture slightly different. Note: a dedicated plantain ingredient should be added to the master ingredient table.

content.text: Fried plantain is the easy side that appears with almost everything in the Caribbean kitchen. The right plantain for this is fully ripe: the skin should be black or heavily speckled, and the flesh inside will be deep yellow-orange and very sweet. Green plantain would fry up starchy and bland; black-skinned is the target. If plantain isn't available, very ripe banana works and produces a sweeter, softer result.

The method is straightforward. Diagonal slices fry faster than rounds and have more surface area for caramelisation. A moderate oil temperature means the outside colours slowly while the inside has time to warm through; too hot and the surface browns before the centre softens.

items.prepNote: ripe plantain — use very ripe (black-skinned) plantain if available, or ripe banana as a substitute
about 5 mm depth in the pan
optional — omit for a sweeter result

content.text: Peel the plantain or banana and cut on the diagonal into slices about 1 cm thick. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. When the oil shimmers, add the slices in a single layer. Fry for 2–3 minutes until the underside is golden-brown and caramelised. Turn with tongs and fry the other side for another 2 minutes.

Lift the slices onto kitchen paper to drain for 30 seconds. A pinch of salt over the top at this stage brings out the sweetness rather than salting the flavour. Serve immediately — fried plantain loses its contrast between the caramelised exterior and the soft interior as it cools.

items.cause: Oil too hot; very ripe plantain has a high sugar content that colours fast
items.fix: Lower the heat to medium-low. The oil should be shimmering, not smoking

items.symptom: Plantain falling apart in the pan
items.cause: Overripe to the point of being mushy; slices too thin
items.fix: Use plantain or banana that is black-skinned but still firm to the touch. Cut slices at least 8 mm thick

items.symptom: Banana not as sweet as expected
items.cause: Not ripe enough; banana is a milder substitute for plantain
items.fix: Use the ripest possible banana, with brown spots throughout the skin. Plantain from a Caribbean grocer will give a more robust flavour and firmer texture than banana

items.cause: Too much oil or not drained sufficiently
items.fix: Use 5 mm of oil rather than deep frying. Drain on kitchen paper for at least 60 seconds before serving

content.text: Fried plantain appears across West Africa, the Caribbean, and Central and South America. In the Caribbean kitchen, it sits alongside rice and peas, jerk chicken, or ackee and saltfish as a sweet counterpoint to the spice and salt of the main dish. The version here is the sweet, ripe type: if you want the crisp, savoury style, use green plantain and make tostones instead. Both versions use the same ingredient at different stages of ripeness, which gives a completely different result.
